title: CLAUDE.md
text: fmrilss R package implementing efficient Least Squares Separate (LSS) analysis functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI) data. LSS used estimate trial--trial activation patterns event-related fMRI designs, critical multivariate pattern analysis (MVPA) connectivity studies. package provides multiple backends, R implementations highly optimized C++ OpenMP parallelization.

title: CLAUDE.md
text: package provides multiple implementations LSS algorithm, accessible unified interface: - r_optimized: Optimized R implementation (default, recommended) - cpp_optimized: Parallelized C++ using OpenMP (fastest large datasets) - r_vectorized: Standard vectorized R - cpp: Standard C++ implementation - naive: Simple loop-based R (reference implementation testing) - oasis: Mathematically equivalent reformulation ridge regularization support Key design pattern: methods use lss(Y, X, Z, Nuisance) signature : - Y: data matrix (timepoints x voxels) - X: trial design matrix (one column per trial) - Z: experimental regressors (intercept, trends, blocks) - Nuisance: regressors project (motion, physiology)

title: CLAUDE.md
text: OASIS Method: Reformulates LSS single matrix operation, eliminating per-trial GLM redundancy. Supports: - Multi-basis HRF models (K > 1) - Ridge regularization (absolute fractional modes) - Automatic design construction event onsets via fmrihrf - Standard error computation - AR(1) prewhitening Voxel-wise HRF Estimation: estimate_voxel_hrf() fits per-voxel HRF basis coefficients, enabling data-driven HRF modeling LSS. Shared-Basis HRF Models (SBHM): Learn low-rank shared time bases parameterized HRF libraries via SVD, enabling efficient multi-voxel HRF estimation reduced parameters. Prewhitening: Integration fmriAR package AR/ARMA noise modeling flexible pooling strategies (global, voxel-wise, run-aware, parcel-based).

title: Getting started with fmrilss
text: rapid event-related fMRI, hemodynamic responses consecutive trials overlap, making trial--trial beta estimates unstable. problem MVPA, RSA, connectivity analyses need reliable single-trial activation patterns. Least Squares Separate (LSS; Mumford et al., 2012) solves fitting separate GLM trial: one regressor trial interest, one aggregating trials, plus nuisance regressors. reduces collinearity stabilizes estimates. fmrilss provides optimized LSS implementations (R C++ OpenMP), plus extensions automatic HRF estimation, ridge regularization, OASIS single-pass solver.

title: Getting started with fmrilss
code: library(fmrihrf) library(fmrilss) set.seed(42)

title: Getting started with fmrilss
text: ’ll simulate 12 trials 150-TR run jittered onsets, convolve canonical HRF build one design-matrix column per trial. X 150 x 12 matrix — one column per trial, containing HRF-convolved impulse.
code: n_time <- 150 n_trials <- 12 n_vox <- 25 sframe <- sampling_frame(blocklens = n_time, TR = 1)  base <- round(seq(10, n_time - 24, length.out = n_trials)) onsets <- sort(pmax(10, pmin(base + sample(-3:3, n_trials, TRUE), n_time - 24))) rset <- regressor_set(onsets, factor(seq_along(onsets)),                       hrf = HRF_SPMG1, duration = 0, span = 24, summate = FALSE) X <- evaluate(rset, grid = samples(sframe, global = TRUE),               precision = 0.1, method = "conv")

title: Getting started with fmrilss
code: Z <- cbind(1, scale(seq_len(n_time)))       # intercept + linear trend Nuisance <- matrix(rnorm(n_time * 6), n_time, 6)  # e.g. motion

what: Simulate data with known ground truth
title: Getting started with fmrilss
code: true_betas <- matrix(rnorm(n_trials * n_vox, 0, 1.2), n_trials, n_vox) Y <- X %*% true_betas +      Z %*% matrix(rnorm(2 * n_vox), 2) +      Nuisance %*% matrix(rnorm(6 * n_vox), 6) +      matrix(rnorm(n_time * n_vox), n_time, n_vox) stopifnot(   all(dim(Y) == c(n_time, n_vox)),   all(is.finite(Y)) )

title: Getting started with fmrilss
text: simplest, lss() needs data trial design: Include Z Nuisance complete model: ’s — beta_full 12 x 25 matrix trial--voxel estimates.
code: beta <- lss(Y, X) dim(beta) #> [1] 12 25 beta_full <- lss(Y, X, Z = Z, Nuisance = Nuisance) stopifnot(   all(dim(beta_full) == c(n_trials, n_vox)),   all(is.finite(beta_full)) )

title: Getting started with fmrilss
text: traditional approach (“Least Squares ”, LSA) estimates trials one model. Let’s compare: simulation, LSS less variable closer known trial effects. scatter plot shows two estimators relate :
code: beta_lsa <- lsa(Y, X, Z = Z, Nuisance = Nuisance) comparison_summary <- data.frame(   Method = c("LSS", "LSA"),   CorrelationToTruth = c(     cor(as.vector(beta_full), as.vector(true_betas)),     cor(as.vector(beta_lsa), as.vector(true_betas))   ),   MeanTrialVariance = c(     mean(apply(beta_full, 2, var)),     mean(apply(beta_lsa, 2, var))   ) ) comparison_summary #>   Method CorrelationToTruth MeanTrialVariance #> 1    LSS          0.9602123          1.675530 #> 2    LSA          0.8996540          2.163055 stopifnot(all(is.finite(as.matrix(comparison_summary[, -1])))) plot(as.vector(beta_lsa), as.vector(beta_full),      pch = 19, col = adjustcolor("steelblue", 0.3), cex = 0.6,      xlab = "LSA beta", ylab = "LSS beta",      main = "Trial-wise betas: LSS vs LSA") abline(0, 1, lty = 2, col = "gray40")

title: Getting started with fmrilss
text: fMRI time series temporal autocorrelation. Pass prewhiten list correct : Automatic AR order selection: See ?lss additional pooling strategies ("voxel", "run", "parcel") ARMA models.
code: beta_ar1 <- lss(Y, X, Z = Z, Nuisance = Nuisance,                 prewhiten = list(method = "ar", p = 1)) beta_auto <- lss(Y, X, Z = Z, Nuisance = Nuisance,                  prewhiten = list(method = "ar", p = "auto", p_max = 4))

title: Getting started with fmrilss
text: default backend ("r_optimized") fast readable. large datasets, switch parallelized C++ engine: Available methods: "naive", "r_vectorized", "r_optimized" (default), "cpp_optimized", "oasis".
code: beta_cpp <- lss(Y, X, Z = Z, Nuisance = Nuisance, method = "cpp_optimized") all.equal(beta_full, beta_cpp, tolerance = 1e-8) #> [1] TRUE

title: Getting started with fmrilss
text: OASIS can build design matrix event specifications, add ridge regularization, fit multi-basis HRFs — one call: Setting X = NULL lets OASIS construct design internally design_spec. Fractional ridge regularization (5%) applied default stabilize overlapping designs. See vignette("oasis_method") ridge tuning, multi-basis HRFs, standard errors, diagnostics.
code: beta_oasis <- lss(   Y, X = NULL, method = "oasis",   oasis = list(     design_spec = list(       sframe = sframe,       cond = list(onsets = onsets, hrf = HRF_SPMG1, span = 24)     )   ) ) dim(beta_oasis) #> [1] 12 25

title: Getting started with fmrilss
text: plan re-run LSS different settings, can faster project nuisance front: useful sweeping hyperparameters (e.g., ridge values) without re-projecting nuisance regressors time.
code: Q <- project_confounds(Nuisance) beta_pre <- lss(Q %*% Y, Q %*% X, Z = Z, method = "r_optimized")

title: Using fmridesign with fmrilss
text: fmridesign package provides powerful formula-based interface creating fMRI design matrices. fmrilss design_spec format OASIS method, can now use event_model baseline_model objects directly new lss_design() function.

title: Using fmridesign with fmrilss
text: Use lss_design() : multi-condition factorial designs need parametric modulators (e.g., RT, difficulty ratings) want design validation diagnostic tools prefer formula-based specification need explicit multi-run handling run-relative onsets

title: Using fmridesign with fmrilss
text: Use traditional lss() interface : simple trial-wise designs already constructed want minimal dependencies ’re using internal SBHM pipelines already design matrices prepared

title: Using fmridesign with fmrilss
text: demonstrate workflow, ’ll create simulated fMRI data realistic trial effects.
code: set.seed(123)  # Two runs, 150 scans each, TR = 2s n_scans_per_run <- 150 n_runs <- 2 n_voxels <- 500 TR <- 2  # True trial effects (betas) for 6 trials per run true_betas <- matrix(rnorm(12 * n_voxels, mean = 1.5, sd = 0.8),                      nrow = 12, ncol = n_voxels)  # Trial data with run-relative onsets trials <- data.frame(   onset = rep(c(10, 30, 50, 70, 90, 110), times = 2),   run = rep(1:2, each = 6) )  # Sampling frame sframe <- sampling_frame(blocklens = rep(n_scans_per_run, n_runs), TR = TR)  # Create design matrix to generate Y emod_sim <- event_model(   onset ~ trialwise(basis = "spmg1"),   data = trials,   block = ~run,   sampling_frame = sframe )  # Extract trial design matrix and convert to matrix X_trial <- as.matrix(design_matrix(emod_sim))  # Baseline: intercept + linear drift per run bmodel_sim <- baseline_model(   basis = "poly",   degree = 1,   sframe = sframe,   intercept = "runwise" ) Z_baseline <- as.matrix(design_matrix(bmodel_sim))  # Generate Y with signal + noise signal <- X_trial %*% true_betas baseline_signal <- Z_baseline %*% matrix(rnorm(ncol(Z_baseline) * n_voxels, sd = 2),                                          ncol = n_voxels) noise <- matrix(rnorm(nrow(X_trial) * n_voxels, sd = 3),                 nrow = nrow(X_trial), ncol = n_voxels)  Y <- signal + baseline_signal + noise

title: Using fmridesign with fmrilss
text: ’s minimal example using lss_design():
code: # Use the first run only for quick start trials_run1 <- trials[trials$run == 1, ] sframe_run1 <- sampling_frame(blocklens = n_scans_per_run, TR = TR) Y_run1 <- Y[1:n_scans_per_run, ]  # Build event model with trialwise design emod <- event_model(   onset ~ trialwise(basis = "spmg1"),   data = trials_run1,   block = ~run,   sampling_frame = sframe_run1 )  # Run LSS beta <- lss_design(Y_run1, emod, method = "oasis")  # Result: 6 trials × 500 voxels dim(beta) #> [1]   6 500 stopifnot(all(dim(beta) == c(6, n_voxels)), all(is.finite(beta)))

title: Using fmridesign with fmrilss
text: One key advantages lss_design() automatic handling multi-run experiments proper onset timing.

title: Using fmridesign with fmrilss
text: Important: using fmridesign, onsets run-relative (resetting 0 start run). standard convention multi-run fMRI experiments.
code: # Trial data with run-relative onsets (already defined above) print(trials) #>    onset run #> 1     10   1 #> 2     30   1 #> 3     50   1 #> 4     70   1 #> 5     90   1 #> 6    110   1 #> 7     10   2 #> 8     30   2 #> 9     50   2 #> 10    70   2 #> 11    90   2 #> 12   110   2  # Create event model - conversion to global time is automatic emod_multi <- event_model(   onset ~ trialwise(basis = "spmg1"),   data = trials,   block = ~run,   sampling_frame = sframe )  # Run LSS beta_multi <- lss_design(Y, emod_multi, method = "oasis")  # Result: 12 trials × 500 voxels dim(beta_multi) #> [1]  12 500

title: Using fmridesign with fmrilss
text: Standard convention: experiment software (E-Prime, PsychoPy) logs onsets relative run start Easier data management: manual offset calculations needed Automatic conversion: fmridesign handles conversion global time internally Less error-prone: Reduces risk incorrect timing specifications

title: Using fmridesign with fmrilss
text: baseline_model allows specify drift correction, block intercepts, nuisance regressors structured way.
code: # Create baseline model with B-spline drift correction bmodel <- baseline_model(   basis = "bs",   degree = 5,   sframe = sframe,   intercept = "runwise" )  # LSS with baseline correction beta_baseline <- lss_design(Y, emod_multi, bmodel, method = "oasis")  dim(beta_baseline) #> [1]  12 500

title: Using fmridesign with fmrilss
text: demonstration, ’ll create synthetic motion regressors.
code: # Simulate motion parameters (6 motion parameters per run) motion_run1 <- matrix(rnorm(n_scans_per_run * 6, sd = 0.5),                       nrow = n_scans_per_run, ncol = 6) motion_run2 <- matrix(rnorm(n_scans_per_run * 6, sd = 0.5),                       nrow = n_scans_per_run, ncol = 6)  # Create baseline model with motion as nuisance bmodel_motion <- baseline_model(   basis = "bs",   degree = 5,   sframe = sframe,   intercept = "runwise",   nuisance_list = list(motion_run1, motion_run2) )  beta_motion <- lss_design(Y, emod_multi, bmodel_motion, method = "oasis") dim(beta_motion) #> [1]  12 500 # In real analysis, load motion from files: motion_run1 <- as.matrix(read.table("motion_run1.txt")) motion_run2 <- as.matrix(read.table("motion_run2.txt"))  bmodel <- baseline_model(   basis = "bs",   degree = 5,   sframe = sframe,   intercept = "runwise",   nuisance_list = list(motion_run1, motion_run2) )

title: Using fmridesign with fmrilss
text: multi-basis HRF models (e.g., canonical + temporal + dispersion derivatives), use nbasis:
code: # Create event model with SPMG3 (3 basis functions) emod_3basis <- event_model(   onset ~ trialwise(basis = "spmg3", nbasis = 3),   data = trials,   block = ~run,   sampling_frame = sframe )  # LSS will auto-detect K = 3 beta_3basis <- lss_design(Y, emod_3basis, method = "oasis")  # Output: (12 trials × 3 basis) × 500 voxels = 36 × 500 dim(beta_3basis) #> [1]  36 500  # Extract canonical basis estimates (every 3rd row starting at 1) beta_canonical <- beta_3basis[seq(1, nrow(beta_3basis), by = 3), ] dim(beta_canonical) #> [1]  12 500

title: Using fmridesign with fmrilss
text: designs potential collinearity, use ridge regularization:
code: beta_ridge <- lss_design(   Y, emod_multi, bmodel,   method = "oasis",   oasis = list(     ridge_mode = "fractional",     ridge_x = 0.02,     ridge_b = 0.02   ) )  dim(beta_ridge) #> [1]  12 500

title: Using fmridesign with fmrilss
text: Limitations: - Requires global/absolute onsets (run-relative) - structured baseline handling - multi-condition support - parametric modulators - Less validation
code: # Manual design_spec construction requires global/absolute onsets # For run-relative onsets (10, 30, 50, 70, 90, 110) in each of 2 runs, # global onsets would be: 10, 30, 50, 70, 90, 110, 310, 330, 350, 370, 390, 410 # (second run starts at 150 scans × 2s = 300s)  beta_old <- lss(   Y,   method = "oasis",   oasis = list(     design_spec = list(       sframe = sframe,       cond = list(         onsets = c(10, 30, 50, 70, 90, 110, 310, 330, 350, 370, 390, 410),         hrf = HRF_SPMG1,         span = 30       )     )   ) )

title: Using fmridesign with fmrilss
text: Advantages: - Run-relative onsets (standard convention) - Structured baseline (drift + nuisance) - Automatic validation - Richer metadata - Formula-based DSL
code: # Formula-based design with run-relative onsets emod_new <- event_model(   onset ~ trialwise(basis = "spmg1"),   data = trials,   block = ~run,   sampling_frame = sframe )  bmodel_new <- baseline_model(basis = "bs", degree = 5, sframe = sframe)  beta_new <- lss_design(Y, emod_new, bmodel_new, method = "oasis")

title: Using fmridesign with fmrilss
text: Let’s visualize well LSS recovers true trial effects simulated data.  fit perfect simulation adds substantial baseline structure noise, recovered betas still track ground truth way easy diagnose numerically visually.
code: # Compare estimated betas (with baseline correction) to true betas # Flatten matrices for plotting est_vec <- as.vector(beta_baseline) true_vec <- as.vector(true_betas)  recovery_summary <- data.frame(   Correlation = cor(est_vec, true_vec),   RMSE = sqrt(mean((est_vec - true_vec)^2)) ) recovery_summary #>   Correlation     RMSE #> 1   0.5201957 1.251591 stopifnot(all(is.finite(as.matrix(recovery_summary))))  # Plot plot(true_vec, est_vec,      pch = 16, cex = 0.3, col = rgb(0, 0, 0, 0.3),      xlab = "True Beta", ylab = "Estimated Beta",      main = sprintf("LSS Recovery (r = %.3f)", recovery_summary$Correlation)) abline(0, 1, col = "red", lwd = 2, lty = 2) grid()

title: Using fmridesign with fmrilss
text: event_model supports parametric modulators trial--trial amplitude modulation. example demonstrates syntax requires special setup.
code: # Trial data with reaction times trials_rt <- data.frame(   onset = c(10, 30, 50, 70, 90, 110),   RT = c(0.5, 0.7, 0.6, 0.8, 0.5, 0.9),   run = 1 )  # Center RT trials_rt$RT_c <- scale(trials_rt$RT, center = TRUE, scale = FALSE)[, 1]  # Model: trial effects + RT modulation emod_rt <- event_model(   onset ~ trialwise() + hrf(RT_c),   data = trials_rt,   block = ~run,   sampling_frame = sframe )  # Note: This creates trial-wise regressors PLUS an RT amplitude modulator # May require special handling in OASIS for proper separation

what: Error: “Y has X rows but sampling_frame expects Y scans”
title: Using fmridesign with fmrilss
text: Cause: Mismatch data dimensions sampling_frame specification. Solution: Check sum(blocklens) matches nrow(Y):
code: sframe_check <- sampling_frame(blocklens = c(150, 150), TR = 2) sum(fmrihrf::blocklens(sframe_check)) #> [1] 300 nrow(Y) #> [1] 300

what: Error: “event_model and baseline_model have different sampling_frames”
title: Using fmridesign with fmrilss
text: Cause: two models created different sampling_frame objects. Solution: Use sframe object :
code: sframe <- sampling_frame(blocklens = c(150, 150), TR = 2)  emod <- event_model(..., sampling_frame = sframe) bmodel <- baseline_model(..., sframe = sframe)

title: Using fmridesign with fmrilss
text: Cause: Events close together design ill-conditioned. Solution: Use ridge regularization:
code: beta <- lss_design(   Y, emod, bmodel,   oasis = list(ridge_mode = "fractional", ridge_x = 0.02) )

title: Using fmridesign with fmrilss
text: lss_design() function provides modern, formula-based interface LSS analysis : Handles multi-run experiments correctly run-relative onsets Provides structured baseline specification Supports multi-basis HRFs automatic detection Validates design compatibility automatically Integrates fmridesign ecosystem simple designs already design matrices prepared, traditional lss() interface remains fully supported unchanged.

what: What OASIS Adds Over Plain LSS
title: The OASIS Method: Optimized Analytic Single-pass Inverse Solution
text: call lss() single-basis HRF ridge penalty, optimized backends already factor shared structure across trials single-pass solve. OASIS wraps estimator richer workflow. get automatic design construction event onsets via fmrihrf, multi-basis HRF support (K > 1), ridge regularization, analytical standard errors, optional AR(1) prewhitening—one call. need leanest single-basis path, stick method = "cpp_optimized".

title: The OASIS Method: Optimized Analytic Single-pass Inverse Solution
text: generate rapid event-related dataset jittered ISIs known ground-truth betas.
code: n_time   <- 300 n_voxels <- 100 TR       <- 1.0 sframe   <- fmrihrf::sampling_frame(blocklens = n_time, TR = TR) isi    <- runif(500, min = 3, max = 9) onsets <- cumsum(c(10, isi)) onsets <- onsets[onsets < (n_time - 20)] n_trials <- length(onsets) true_betas <- matrix(rnorm(n_trials * n_voxels, mean = 1, sd = 0.5),                      n_trials, n_voxels) grid <- fmrihrf::samples(sframe, global = TRUE) rset <- fmrihrf::regressor_set(   onsets = onsets, fac = factor(seq_len(n_trials)),   hrf = fmrihrf::HRF_SPMG1, duration = 0, span = 30, summate = FALSE ) X_trials <- fmrihrf::evaluate(rset, grid = grid, precision = 0.1, method = "conv") Y <- matrix(rnorm(n_time * n_voxels), n_time, n_voxels) Y <- Y + X_trials %*% true_betas

title: The OASIS Method: Optimized Analytic Single-pass Inverse Solution
text: Pass design_spec instead pre-built X let OASIS construct trial-wise design internally. result n_trials x n_voxels matrix, exactly shape get LSS backends. default, OASIS applies 5% fractional ridge regularization, worth keeping mind compare unregularized LSS fits.
code: beta_oasis <- lss(   Y = Y, X = NULL, method = "oasis",   oasis = list(design_spec = list(     sframe = sframe,     cond   = list(onsets = onsets, hrf = fmrihrf::HRF_SPMG1, span = 30)   )) ) dim(beta_oasis) #> [1]  42 100

title: The OASIS Method: Optimized Analytic Single-pass Inverse Solution
text: single-basis HRF ridge penalty, OASIS returns coefficients optimized LSS path. can verify floating-point tolerance. Use plain LSS backends fixed single-basis X want leanest dependency surface. Prefer OASIS need built-design construction, multi-basis HRFs, ridge, standard errors.
code: Z <- cbind(1, scale(1:n_time)) b_lss   <- lss(Y, X_trials, Z = Z, method = "cpp_optimized") b_oasis <- lss(Y, X_trials, Z = Z, method = "oasis",                oasis = list(ridge_mode = "absolute", ridge_x = 0, ridge_b = 0)) equiv_err <- max(abs(b_lss - b_oasis)) equiv_err stopifnot(equiv_err < 1e-10)

title: The OASIS Method: Optimized Analytic Single-pass Inverse Solution
text: rapid designs, close trial spacing produces correlated regressors. Ridge regression trades small bias large variance reduction. OASIS offers two modes.

title: The OASIS Method: Optimized Analytic Single-pass Inverse Solution
text: specify fixed penalty values added diagonal per-trial normal equations.
code: beta_noridge <- lss(   Y = Y, X = NULL, method = "oasis",   oasis = list(     design_spec = list(sframe = sframe,       cond = list(onsets = onsets, hrf = fmrihrf::HRF_SPMG1, span = 30)),     ridge_mode = "absolute", ridge_x = 0, ridge_b = 0   ) )  beta_abs <- lss(   Y = Y, X = NULL, method = "oasis",   oasis = list(     design_spec = list(sframe = sframe,       cond = list(onsets = onsets, hrf = fmrihrf::HRF_SPMG1, span = 30)),     ridge_mode = "absolute", ridge_x = 0.1, ridge_b = 0.1   ) )

title: The OASIS Method: Optimized Analytic Single-pass Inverse Solution
text: penalty scales relative mean diagonal energy design, adapting automatically data. 5% fractional ridge also package default override ridge settings. Notice variance reduction compared unregularized estimates: Practical starting points fractional ridge: 0.005 well-separated trials (ISI >= 6 s), 0.01 typical rapid designs, 0.02–0.05 dense events multi-basis models. See ?lss details.
code: beta_frac <- lss(   Y = Y, X = NULL, method = "oasis",   oasis = list(     design_spec = list(sframe = sframe,       cond = list(onsets = onsets, hrf = fmrihrf::HRF_SPMG1, span = 30)),     ridge_mode = "fractional", ridge_x = 0.05, ridge_b = 0.05   ) ) ridge_summary <- data.frame(   Fit = c("No ridge", "Fractional 5%"),   MeanTrialVariance = c(     mean(apply(beta_noridge, 2, var)),     mean(apply(beta_frac, 2, var))   ) ) ridge_summary #>             Fit MeanTrialVariance #> 1      No ridge         0.3679561 #> 2 Fractional 5%         0.3330022 stopifnot(ridge_summary$MeanTrialVariance[2] < ridge_summary$MeanTrialVariance[1])

title: The OASIS Method: Optimized Analytic Single-pass Inverse Solution
text: Multi-basis models capture trial--trial variability HRF shape. OASIS returns K rows per trial, interleaved basis. K = 3 (canonical + temporal derivative + dispersion derivative), output K * n_trials rows. Extract component using K-stride pattern. Large temporal-derivative weights suggest timing variability across trials; dispersion-derivative weights indicate width changes.
code: beta_spmg3 <- lss(   Y = Y, X = NULL, method = "oasis",   oasis = list(design_spec = list(     sframe = sframe,     cond = list(onsets = onsets, hrf = fmrihrf::HRF_SPMG3, span = 30)   )) ) dim(beta_spmg3) #> [1] 126 100 K <- 3 canonical  <- beta_spmg3[seq(1, nrow(beta_spmg3), by = K), ] temporal   <- beta_spmg3[seq(2, nrow(beta_spmg3), by = K), ] dispersion <- beta_spmg3[seq(3, nrow(beta_spmg3), by = K), ] cat("Each component:", nrow(canonical), "trials x", ncol(canonical), "voxels\n") #> Each component: 42 trials x 100 voxels

title: The OASIS Method: Optimized Analytic Single-pass Inverse Solution
text: Finite Impulse Response basis makes parametric assumptions HRF shape. trial contributes one coefficient per time bin. Moderate ridge important FIR fits high-dimensional. can average across trials voxels recover smooth HRF estimate.
code: fir_hrf  <- fmrihrf::hrf_fir_generator(nbasis = 15, span = 30) beta_fir <- lss(   Y = Y, X = NULL, method = "oasis",   oasis = list(     design_spec = list(sframe = sframe,       cond = list(onsets = onsets, hrf = fir_hrf, span = 30)),     ridge_mode = "fractional", ridge_x = 0.2, ridge_b = 0.2   ) ) dim(beta_fir) #> [1] 630 100 n_bins    <- 15 bin_width <- 30 / n_bins fir_array <- array(beta_fir, dim = c(n_bins, n_trials, n_voxels)) fir_mean  <- apply(fir_array, 1, mean) fir_se    <- apply(fir_array, 1, sd) / sqrt(n_trials * n_voxels) tp        <- seq(0, (n_bins - 1) * bin_width, by = bin_width) plot(tp, fir_mean, type = "l", col = "navy", lwd = 2,      main = "FIR-derived HRF", xlab = "Time (s)", ylab = "Response") polygon(c(tp, rev(tp)), c(fir_mean + fir_se, rev(fir_mean - fir_se)),         col = grDevices::adjustcolor("navy", 0.2), border = NA)

title: The OASIS Method: Optimized Analytic Single-pass Inverse Solution
text: unsure HRF shape fits best, pass candidate grid via hrf_grid let OASIS pick single global winner using matched-filter score. selects single HRF shared across voxels, refits . voxel-specific HRF estimation, see vignette("voxel-wise-hrf").
code: hrf_grid <- create_lwu_grid(   tau_range = c(4, 8), sigma_range = c(2, 3.5),   rho_range = c(0.2, 0.5), n_tau = 3, n_sigma = 2, n_rho = 2 )  beta_grid <- lss(   Y = Y, X = NULL, method = "oasis",   oasis = list(     design_spec = list(sframe = sframe,       cond = list(onsets = onsets, span = 30),       hrf_grid = hrf_grid$hrfs),     ridge_mode = "fractional", ridge_x = 0.01, ridge_b = 0.01   ) ) dim(beta_grid)

title: The OASIS Method: Optimized Analytic Single-pass Inverse Solution
text: Real experiments often multiple conditions. Place target condition cond conditions others variance accounted nuisance. others list ensures condition B’s variance accounted estimating condition betas, preventing omitted-variable bias.
code: onsets_a <- seq(10, 280, by = 30) onsets_b <- seq(25, 280, by = 30)  beta_multi <- lss(   Y = Y, X = NULL, method = "oasis",   oasis = list(design_spec = list(     sframe = sframe,     cond   = list(onsets = onsets_a, hrf = fmrihrf::HRF_SPMG1, span = 30),     others = list(list(onsets = onsets_b))   )) ) dim(beta_multi) #> [1]  10 100

title: The OASIS Method: Optimized Analytic Single-pass Inverse Solution
text: Request analytical standard errors return_se = TRUE. can compute trial-level t-statistics directly. Trials larger standard errors typically overlapping neighbours occur noisier periods.
code: res_se <- lss(   Y = Y[, 1:10], X = NULL, method = "oasis",   oasis = list(     design_spec = list(sframe = sframe,       cond = list(onsets = onsets, hrf = fmrihrf::HRF_SPMG1, span = 30)),     return_se = TRUE   ) ) t_stats <- res_se$beta / res_se$se cat("Mean |t|:", round(mean(abs(t_stats)), 2), "\n") #> Mean |t|: 2.77 stopifnot(   all(dim(res_se$beta) == dim(res_se$se)),   all(is.finite(res_se$se)) )

title: The OASIS Method: Optimized Analytic Single-pass Inverse Solution
text: fMRI time series temporally autocorrelated. can apply AR(1) prewhitening standard errors t-statistics valid. OASIS applies whitening transform consistently data, trial design, nuisance regressors. advanced options (auto AR order selection, voxel-wise parcel-based pooling, ARMA models), see ?lss.
code: beta_pw <- lss(   Y = Y[, 1:10], X = NULL, method = "oasis",   oasis = list(design_spec = list(sframe = sframe,     cond = list(onsets = onsets, hrf = fmrihrf::HRF_SPMG1, span = 30))),   prewhiten = list(method = "ar", p = 1) ) dim(beta_pw) #> [1] 42 10

title: The OASIS Method: Optimized Analytic Single-pass Inverse Solution
text: Prefer OASIS want HRF-aware design construction, multi-basis HRFs (K > 1), ridge regularization, standard errors one call. especially useful rapid event-related designs hundreds trials. Prefer standard LSS backends already single-basis design matrix X want leanest, transparent code path.

title: OASIS Theory: Algebra and Implementation Details
text: Optimized Analytic Single-pass Inverse Solution (OASIS) extends Least Squares Separate (LSS) estimation algebraic reformulation enables single-pass computation trial estimates. single-basis HRF (K = 1) without ridge, OASIS reduces exactly closed-form LSS solution; per‑trial 2x2 normal equations . value OASIS batching solves efficiently generalizing algebra multi‑basis HRFs (2Kx2K) optional ridge diagnostics. document provides mathematical foundation implementation details. Read vignette("oasis_method") want linear algebra behind user-facing API. code chunks make main scaling claims executable rather purely narrative.

title: OASIS Theory: Algebra and Implementation Details
text: vignette assumes familiarity : - QR decomposition orthogonal projection matrices - Ridge regression regularization - Matrix calculus linear algebra - standard LSS formulation

title: OASIS Theory: Algebra and Implementation Details
text: Standard LSS requires N separate GLM fits N trials, involving: 1. Matrix assembly: O(T²) operations 2. QR decomposition: O(T³) operations 3. Back-substitution: O(T²) operations Total complexity: O(NT³) N trials OASIS recognizes N models share substantial structure. factoring common computations, OASIS reduces complexity : 1. Single QR decomposition: O(T³) 2. Shared projections: O(NT²) 3. Per-trial solutions: O(N) Total complexity: O(T³ + NT²), significant reduction N large.

title: OASIS Theory: Algebra and Implementation Details
text: Computational complexity: Classical LSS vs OASIS Code references point R/oasis_glue.R src/oasis_core.cpp implementations. Notation used throughout: Y∈ℝT×VY \\mathbb{R}^{T \times V}: voxel data (TT time points, VV voxels) X=[x1,…,xN]∈ℝT×NX = [x_1, \dots, x_N] \\mathbb{R}^{T \times N}: trial regressors one condition Z∈ℝT×KzZ \\mathbb{R}^{T \times K_z}: nuisance/experimental regressors shared across trials R=−QQTR = - QQ^T: orthogonal projector removing nuisance effects (QQ comes QR factorisation [Z,others][Z,\text{others}]) Inner products denoted ⟨,b⟩=aTb\langle , b \rangle = ^T b first treat single-basis case (one regressor per trial) generalizing multi-basis HRFs.
code: # Demonstrate computational scaling N_trials <- c(10, 50, 100, 200, 500, 1000) T_points <- 200  # Fixed number of timepoints  # Simplified complexity models (arbitrary units) classical_ops <- N_trials * T_points^3 / 1e6  # O(NT³) oasis_ops <- (T_points^3 + N_trials * T_points^2) / 1e6  # O(T³ + NT²)  # Create comparison plot plot(N_trials, classical_ops, type='l', col='red', lwd=2,      xlab='Number of Trials', ylab='Computational Operations (millions)',      main='Computational Complexity: Classical LSS vs OASIS',      ylim=c(0, max(classical_ops))) lines(N_trials, oasis_ops, col='blue', lwd=2) legend('topleft', c('Classical LSS', 'OASIS'),        col=c('red', 'blue'), lwd=2, bty='n')  # Add shaded region showing computational savings polygon(c(N_trials, rev(N_trials)),         c(classical_ops, rev(oasis_ops)),         col=rgb(0.2, 0.8, 0.2, 0.3), border=NA) text(500, mean(c(classical_ops[4], oasis_ops[4])),      'Computational\nSavings', col='darkgreen') complexity_summary <- data.frame(   Trials = N_trials,   Classical = classical_ops,   OASIS = oasis_ops,   SpeedupRatio = classical_ops / oasis_ops ) complexity_summary #>   Trials Classical OASIS SpeedupRatio #> 1     10        80   8.4      9.52381 #> 2     50       400  10.0     40.00000 #> 3    100       800  12.0     66.66667 #> 4    200      1600  16.0    100.00000 #> 5    500      4000  28.0    142.85714 #> 6   1000      8000  48.0    166.66667 stopifnot(all(complexity_summary$SpeedupRatio > 1))

title: OASIS Theory: Algebra and Implementation Details
text: Classical LSS fits, trial jj, GLM design [xj,bj,Z][x_j, b_j, Z], bj=∑≠jxib_j = \sum_{\neq j} x_i. Solving model independently costs 𝒪(N)\mathcal{O}(N) QR factorizations. Algebraically, trial-specific beta can expressed β̂j=⟨Rxj,RY⟩−⟨Rxj,Rbj⟩∥Rbj∥2⟨Rbj,RY⟩∥Rxj∥2−⟨Rxj,Rbj⟩2∥Rbj∥2. \hat{\beta}_j = \frac{\langle Rx_j, RY \rangle - \frac{\langle Rx_j, Rb_j \rangle}{\|Rb_j\|^2} \langle Rb_j, RY \rangle}{\|Rx_j\|^2 - \frac{\langle Rx_j, Rb_j \rangle^2}{\|Rb_j\|^2}}. OASIS extracts reuses common computational components (projections, norms, cross-products) across trials, computing .

title: OASIS Theory: Algebra and Implementation Details
text: residualising nuisance regressors define: aj=Rxja_j = Rx_j s=∑j=1Najs = \sum_{j=1}^N a_j dj=∥aj∥2d_j = \|a_j\|^2 αj=⟨aj,s−aj⟩\alpha_j = \langle a_j, s - a_j \rangle sj=∥s−aj∥2s_j = \|s - a_j\|^2 Let njv=⟨aj,RY⋅v⟩n_{jv} = \langle a_j, RY_{\cdot v} \rangle mv=⟨s,RY⋅v⟩m_v = \langle s, RY_{\cdot v} \rangle. pair (βj,γj)(\beta_j, \gamma_j) solving 2x2 system trial jj voxel vv obtained Gj[βjvγjv]=[njvmv−njv],Gj=[dj+λxαjαjsj+λb], G_j \begin{bmatrix} \beta_{jv} \\ \gamma_{jv} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} n_{jv} \\ m_v - n_{jv} \end{bmatrix}, \quad G_j = \begin{bmatrix} d_j + \lambda_x & \alpha_j \\ \alpha_j & s_j + \lambda_b \end{bmatrix}, ridge penalties λx,λb≥0\lambda_x, \lambda_b \ge 0. inverse GjG_j analytic, soβjv=(sj+λb)njv−αj(mv−njv)(dj+λx)(sj+λb)−αj2. \beta_{jv} = \frac{(s_j + \lambda_b) n_{jv} - \alpha_j (m_v - n_{jv})}{(d_j + \lambda_x)(s_j + \lambda_b) - \alpha_j^2}. exactly oasis_betas_closed_form() implements (C++ file src/oasis_core.cpp). precomputation step oasis_precompute_design() produces aj,s,dj,αj,sja_j, s, d_j, \alpha_j, s_j , oasis_AtY_SY_blocked() streams voxels obtain njvn_{jv} mvm_v.

title: OASIS Theory: Algebra and Implementation Details
text: oasis$ridge_mode = "fractional" sets λx=ηx⋅d‾\lambda_x = \eta_x \cdot \bar{d} λb=ηb⋅s‾\lambda_b = \eta_b \cdot \bar{s}, d‾\bar{d} s‾\bar{s} means djd_j sjs_j. helper .oasis_resolve_ridge() implements scaling. Absolute ridge uses supplied values directly.

title: OASIS Theory: Algebra and Implementation Details
text: Given Gj−1G_j^{-1} residual norm ∥RY∥2\|RY\|^2, variance βjv\beta_{jv} Var(β̂jv)=σjv2(Gj−1)11,σjv2=SSEjvdof, \operatorname{Var}(\hat{\beta}_{jv}) = \sigma_{jv}^2 \left( G_j^{-1} \right)_{11}, \quad \sigma_{jv}^2 = \frac{\text{SSE}_{jv}}{\text{dof}}, SSEjv=∥RY⋅v∥2−2(βjvnjv+γjv(mv−njv))+djβjv2+sjγjv2+2αjβjvγjv. \text{SSE}_{jv} = \|RY_{\cdot v}\|^2 - 2 (\beta_{jv} n_{jv} + \gamma_{jv} (m_v - n_{jv})) + d_j \beta_{jv}^2 + s_j \gamma_{jv}^2 + 2 \alpha_j \beta_{jv} \gamma_{jv}. .oasis_se_from_norms() realises computation, reusing njvn_{jv}, mvm_v cached design scalars.

title: OASIS Theory: Algebra and Implementation Details
text: HRF contributes K>1K > 1 basis functions, trial columns Aj∈ℝT×KA_j \\mathbb{R}^{T \times K}. Define S=∑jAjS = \sum_j A_j Dj=AjTAjD_j = A_j^T A_j Cj=AjT(S−Aj)C_j = A_j^T (S - A_j) Ej=(S−Aj)T(S−Aj)E_j = (S - A_j)^T (S - A_j) Per voxel need N1=ATRYN1 = ^T RY (stacked NN blocks size KK) SY=STRYSY = S^T RY. block system [Dj+λxICjCjTEj+λbI][BjvΓjv]=[N1jvSYv−N1jv], \begin{bmatrix} D_j + \lambda_x & C_j \\ C_j^T & E_j + \lambda_b \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} B_{jv} \\ \Gamma_{jv} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} N1_{jv} \\ SY_v - N1_{jv} \end{bmatrix}, Bjv∈ℝKB_{jv} \\mathbb{R}^K. oasisk_betas() solves 2Kx2K system via Cholesky factorisation. Ridge adds λxI\lambda_x λbI\lambda_b block diagonals. Compared single-basis path, shapes cached matrices differ; solve still analytic per trial/voxel block. companion oasisk_betas_se() extends SSE/variance calculation multi-basis case, using building blocks.

title: OASIS Theory: Algebra and Implementation Details
text: OASIS can construct XX fly event specifications. .oasis_build_X_from_events() uses fmrihrf::regressor_set() generate trial-wise columns (optional -condition aggregates) given: cond$onsets: per-trial onset times cond$hrf: HRF object (canonical, FIR, multi-basis, user-defined) cond$span, precision, method: convolution controls design residualised nuisance regressors fed algebra . HRF definition enters directly, switching HRFs running grid searches automatically regenerates matching design. provide explicit X, OASIS skips step assumes already encoded HRF matrix.

title: OASIS Theory: Algebra and Implementation Details
text: oasis$whiten = "ar1" estimates common AR(1) coefficient residualised data. .oasis_ar1_whitener() computes ρ\rho applies Toeplitz-safe differencing: ỹt={1−ρ2y1t=1,yt−ρyt−1t>1. \tilde{y}_t = \begin{cases} \sqrt{1 - \rho^2} y_1 & t = 1, \\ y_t - \rho y_{t-1} & t > 1. \end{cases} transformation applied XX nuisance regressors standard OASIS algebra runs. Whitening preserves single-pass benefits transformed data treated exactly like original inputs.

title: OASIS Theory: Algebra and Implementation Details
text: oasis$return_diag = TRUE, OASIS returns precomputed design scalars: Single-basis: dj,αj,sjd_j, \alpha_j, s_j (oasis_precompute_design()) Multi-basis: Dj,Cj,EjD_j, C_j, E_j (oasisk_precompute_design()) matrices useful checking trial collinearity, energy, effect ridge scaling.

title: OASIS Theory: Algebra and Implementation Details
text: Putting everything together, single-basis solver proceeds follows: Residualise YY XX nuisance regressors, optionally whitening. Compute aj,s,dj,αj,sja_j, s, d_j, \alpha_j, s_j (oasis_precompute_design). Stream voxels blocks, forming NY=ATRYN_Y = ^T RY SY=sTRYS_Y = s^T RY (oasis_AtY_SY_blocked). Apply ridge scaling (absolute fractional) obtain λx,λb\lambda_x, \lambda_b. trial, evaluate closed-form βjv\beta_{jv} (γjv\gamma_{jv} SEs requested). Optionally compute SEs diagnostics. multi-basis path swaps steps 2–5 block equivalents. cases, cost dominated single projection YY matrix–vector multiplies step 3, giving 𝒪(TV)\mathcal{O}(T V) complexity small trial-dependent overhead.

title: OASIS Theory: Algebra and Implementation Details
text: Projection / whitening: 𝒪(TVKz)\mathcal{O}(T V K_z) arithmetic, 𝒪(TKz)\mathcal{O}(T K_z) memory confounds Precomputation: 𝒪(TN)\mathcal{O}(T N) Products (blocked): 𝒪(TV)\mathcal{O}(T V) block size tuning Closed-form solves: 𝒪(NV)\mathcal{O}(N V) negligible constants (2x2 2Kx2K systems) Compared classical LSS (NN separate regressions), OASIS shaves repeated projections linear solves, yielding substantial speedups NN VV large.

title: Shared-Basis HRF Matching (SBHM): Efficient Voxel-Specific HRF Estimation
text: Real fMRI data shows substantial HRF variability across brain regions. single canonical HRF often fits poorly, estimating fully unconstrained voxel-wise HRFs expensive noisy. SBHM offers middle path: learn shared basis library plausible HRFs, match voxel best library member low-dimensional space. pipeline four steps: Build shared basis HRF library via SVD (sbhm_build()) Prepass aggregate model fit per voxel (sbhm_prepass()) Match voxels library members cosine similarity (sbhm_match()) Project trial-wise coefficients scalar amplitudes (sbhm_project()) practice, lss_sbhm() runs four steps single call.
code: library(fmrihrf) library(fmrilss)  # Derived convenience defaults for examples n_voxels_default <- if (fast_mode) 20 else 50 n_trials_default <- if (fast_mode) 6 else 10 ranks_default    <- if (fast_mode) 2:6 else 2:10

title: Shared-Basis HRF Matching (SBHM): Efficient Voxel-Specific HRF Estimation
text: start defining grid gamma HRF parameters spanning physiological variability peak time width. grid row becomes gamma HRF. wrap factory function sbhm_build() can evaluate across grid. Now build SBHM basis. SVD decomposes library shared time basis B, singular values S, per-HRF coordinates .
code:
shapes <- if (fast_mode) seq(6, 10, by = 2) else seq(5, 11, by = 1.5) rates  <- if (fast_mode) seq(0.8, 1.2, by = 0.2) else seq(0.7, 1.3, by = 0.15) param_grid <- expand.grid(shape = shapes, rate = rates) cat("Library size:", nrow(param_grid), "HRFs
") #> Library size: 9 HRFs gamma_fun <- function(shape, rate) {   f <- function(t) fmrihrf::hrf_gamma(t, shape = shape, rate = rate)   fmrihrf::as_hrf(f, name = sprintf("gamma(s=%.2f,r=%.2f)", shape, rate), span = 32) } sframe <- sampling_frame(blocklens = n_time, TR = TR) sbhm <- sbhm_build(   library_spec = list(fun = gamma_fun, pgrid = param_grid, span = 32,                       precision = 0.1, method = "conv"),   r = 6, sframe = sframe,   baseline = c(0, 0.5), normalize = TRUE, ref = "mean" ) cat("B (time basis):", dim(sbhm$B), "
") #> B (time basis): 160 6 cat("A (library coords):", dim(sbhm$A), "
") #> A (library coords): 6 9 cat("Singular values:", round(sbhm$S, 2), "
") #> Singular values: 2.68 1.23 0.52 0.17 0.04 0

title: Shared-Basis HRF Matching (SBHM): Efficient Voxel-Specific HRF Estimation
text: basis function captures principal mode HRF variation: main shape, timing shifts, width differences, undershoot features.
code: par(mfrow = c(2, 3), mar = c(3, 3, 2, 1)) for (i in 1:ncol(sbhm$B)) {   plot(sbhm$tgrid, sbhm$B[, i], type = "l", col = "navy", lwd = 2,        main = paste0("Basis ", i, " (s=", round(sbhm$S[i], 2), ")"),        xlab = "Time (s)", ylab = "Amplitude")   abline(h = 0, col = "gray", lty = 2) }

title: Shared-Basis HRF Matching (SBHM): Efficient Voxel-Specific HRF Estimation
text: Aim 90–95% variance explained. can sweep ranks find elbow.
code: ranks <- ranks_default ve <- sapply(ranks, function(ri) {   sum(sbhm_build(library_spec = list(fun = gamma_fun, pgrid = param_grid, span = 32),                  r = ri, sframe = sframe, normalize = TRUE)$S^2) }) plot(ranks, ve / max(ve) * 100, type = "b", pch = 19, col = "navy",      lwd = 2, xlab = "Rank (r)", ylab = "Variance Explained (%)",      main = "Choosing SBHM Rank") abline(h = 95, col = "red", lty = 2) grid()

title: Shared-Basis HRF Matching (SBHM): Efficient Voxel-Specific HRF Estimation
text: sample rank-r reconstructed HRFs shows range shapes library spans.
code: H_hat <- sbhm$B %*% sbhm$A sel <- unique(round(seq(1, ncol(H_hat), length.out = min(ncol(H_hat), 12)))) matplot(sbhm$tgrid, H_hat[, sel, drop = FALSE], type = "l", lty = 1,         col = colorRampPalette(c("#6baed6", "#08519c"))(length(sel)),         lwd = 1.5, xlab = "Time (s)", ylab = "Amplitude",         main = "Sample of Library HRFs (rank-r reconstruction)") abline(h = 0, col = "gray80", lty = 2)

title: Shared-Basis HRF Matching (SBHM): Efficient Voxel-Specific HRF Estimation
text: demonstrate recovery, create data voxel uses known HRF library. First, set experimental design. Assign voxel random library HRF build per-trial regressors. Now generate signal. voxel’s response sum per-trial regressors projected voxel’s HRF coordinates, scaled random amplitude, plus noise.
code: n_voxels <- n_voxels_default n_trials <- n_trials_default safe_end <- max(sbhm$tgrid) - 30 onsets <- seq(20, safe_end, length.out = n_trials) set.seed(456) true_hrf_idx <- sample(ncol(sbhm$A), n_voxels, replace = TRUE) design_spec <- list(   sframe = sframe,   cond = list(onsets = onsets, duration = 0, span = 30) ) hrf_B <- sbhm_hrf(sbhm$B, sbhm$tgrid, sbhm$span) regressors_by_trial <- lapply(onsets, function(ot) {   rr_t <- regressor(onsets = ot, hrf = hrf_B, duration = 0, span = 30, summate = FALSE)   evaluate(rr_t, grid = sbhm$tgrid, precision = 0.1, method = "conv") }) Y <- matrix(rnorm(n_time * n_voxels, sd = 0.5), n_time, n_voxels) true_amplitudes <- matrix(rnorm(n_trials * n_voxels, mean = 2, sd = 0.5),                           n_trials, n_voxels) for (v in 1:n_voxels) {   alpha_true <- sbhm$A[, true_hrf_idx[v]]   for (t in 1:n_trials)     Y[, v] <- Y[, v] + true_amplitudes[t, v] * (regressors_by_trial[[t]] %*% alpha_true) } cat("Y:", dim(Y), "\n") #> Y: 160 20 cat("Unique true HRFs:", length(unique(true_hrf_idx)), "\n") #> Unique true HRFs: 9

title: Shared-Basis HRF Matching (SBHM): Efficient Voxel-Specific HRF Estimation
text: single call lss_sbhm() runs entire pipeline. control lists (prepass, match, oasis, amplitude) optional; pass want override. See ?lss_sbhm full parameter documentation. use fmridesign, prefer lss_sbhm_design() even simpler interface mirrors lss_design(). See ?lss_sbhm_design.
code:
res_sbhm <- lss_sbhm(   Y = Y, sbhm = sbhm, design_spec = design_spec,   return = "both" ) cat("Amplitudes:", dim(res_sbhm$amplitude), "
") #> Amplitudes: 6 20 cat("Coefficients:", dim(res_sbhm$coeffs_r), "
") #> Coefficients: 6 6 20 cat("Matched indices:", length(res_sbhm$matched_idx), "
") #> Matched indices: 20

title: Shared-Basis HRF Matching (SBHM): Efficient Voxel-Specific HRF Estimation
code: accuracy <- mean(res_sbhm$matched_idx == true_hrf_idx) cat("HRF matching accuracy:", round(100 * accuracy, 1), "%\n") #> HRF matching accuracy: 75 % cat("Confused voxels:", sum(res_sbhm$matched_idx != true_hrf_idx),     "/", n_voxels, "\n") #> Confused voxels: 5 / 20

title: Shared-Basis HRF Matching (SBHM): Efficient Voxel-Specific HRF Estimation
text: margin (top-1 minus top-2 cosine score) indicates unambiguous assignment . Higher better.  simulation median margin small, means many voxels sit close multiple library members. exactly regime soft blending (see ) can help.
code: cat("Margin -- mean:", round(mean(res_sbhm$margin), 3),     " median:", round(median(res_sbhm$margin), 3), "\n") #> Margin -- mean: 0.023  median: 0.006 hist(res_sbhm$margin, breaks = 20, col = "skyblue", border = "white",      main = "Matching Confidence (Margin)",      xlab = "Margin (top1 - top2 cosine score)") abline(v = median(res_sbhm$margin), col = "red", lwd = 2, lty = 2) grid()

title: Shared-Basis HRF Matching (SBHM): Efficient Voxel-Specific HRF Estimation
code: cor_amp <- cor(as.vector(res_sbhm$amplitude), as.vector(true_amplitudes)) plot(as.vector(true_amplitudes), as.vector(res_sbhm$amplitude),      pch = 19, col = adjustcolor("navy", alpha.f = 0.3),      xlab = "True Amplitude", ylab = "Estimated Amplitude",      main = paste0("Amplitude Recovery (r = ", round(cor_amp, 3), ")")) abline(0, 1, col = "red", lwd = 2, lty = 2) grid() recovery_summary <- data.frame(   HRFMatchingAccuracy = accuracy,   AmplitudeCorrelation = cor_amp,   MedianMargin = median(res_sbhm$margin) ) recovery_summary #>   HRFMatchingAccuracy AmplitudeCorrelation MedianMargin #> 1                0.75            0.6245209  0.005994378 stopifnot(all(is.finite(as.matrix(recovery_summary))))

title: Shared-Basis HRF Matching (SBHM): Efficient Voxel-Specific HRF Estimation
text: confidently matched voxels, recovered true HRFs overlap closely.
code: H_hat <- sbhm$B %*% sbhm$A vox_show <- head(order(-res_sbhm$margin), n = min(6, n_voxels)) par(mfrow = c(2, 3), mar = c(3, 3, 2, 1)) for (v in vox_show) {   rng <- range(c(H_hat[, true_hrf_idx[v]], H_hat[, res_sbhm$matched_idx[v]]))   plot(sbhm$tgrid, H_hat[, true_hrf_idx[v]], type = "l", col = "#2c7fb8",        lwd = 2, ylim = rng, main = paste0("Voxel ", v), xlab = "Time (s)", ylab = "HRF")   lines(sbhm$tgrid, H_hat[, res_sbhm$matched_idx[v]], col = "#d95f02", lwd = 2, lty = 2)   legend("topright", bty = "n", cex = 0.8, legend = c("True", "Matched"),          lty = 1:2, lwd = 2, col = c("#2c7fb8", "#d95f02")) }

title: Shared-Basis HRF Matching (SBHM): Efficient Voxel-Specific HRF Estimation
text: PCA library coordinates shows HRFs present (true) versus selected (matched).
code: pca <- prcomp(t(sbhm$A), center = TRUE, scale. = TRUE) pc <- pca$x[, 1:2, drop = FALSE] plot(pc, pch = 16, col = "gray70", xlab = "PC1", ylab = "PC2",      main = "Library Coordinate Space (PCA)") points(pc[unique(true_hrf_idx), , drop = FALSE], pch = 1, col = "#2c7fb8", lwd = 2) points(pc[unique(res_sbhm$matched_idx), , drop = FALSE], pch = 4, col = "#d95f02", lwd = 2) legend("topright", bty = "n",        legend = c("Library", "True", "Matched"),        pch = c(16, 1, 4), col = c("gray60", "#2c7fb8", "#d95f02"))

title: Shared-Basis HRF Matching (SBHM): Efficient Voxel-Specific HRF Estimation
text: Hard assignment picks single best HRF per voxel. margin small, blending top-K candidates can reduce variance. built-approach requires just two extra arguments. Blending applies voxels whose margin falls blend_margin; confident voxels keep hard assignment. manual control blending weights, use sbhm_prepass(), sbhm_match(topK = K), sbhm_project() directly. See ?sbhm_match details.
code: res_soft <- lss_sbhm(   Y = Y, sbhm = sbhm, design_spec = design_spec,   match = list(topK = 3, soft_blend = TRUE,                blend_margin = median(res_sbhm$margin)),   return = "amplitude" ) cor_sv <- cor(as.vector(res_soft$amplitude), as.vector(res_sbhm$amplitude)) cat("Soft vs hard amplitude correlation:", round(cor_sv, 3), "\n") #> Soft vs hard amplitude correlation: 0.994

title: Shared-Basis HRF Matching (SBHM): Efficient Voxel-Specific HRF Estimation
text: default, SBHM estimates voxel’s HRF shape aggregate prepass fit (alpha_source = "prepass"). Two alternative strategies available prepass unreliable: "trial_projection" estimates shape per-trial projection coefficients shared basis, using leading singular vector across trials. "oasis_rank1" extracts shape rank-1 approximation trial-wise OASIS betas. Set rank1_min gate voxels low rank-1 variance fraction back prepass.
code: res_proj <- lss_sbhm(   Y, sbhm, design_spec,   match = list(alpha_source = "trial_projection") )

title: Shared-Basis HRF Matching (SBHM): Efficient Voxel-Specific HRF Estimation
text: voxels matching uncertain (low margin weak signal), can gate fallback shape using min_margin min_beta_norm. Gated voxels use reference coordinate instead matched one.
code: res_gated <- lss_sbhm(   Y, sbhm, design_spec,   match = list(min_margin = 0.05, min_beta_norm = 1e-3) ) cat("Voxels gated to fallback:", res_gated$diag$fallback_low_conf_n, "\n")

title: Shared-Basis HRF Matching (SBHM): Efficient Voxel-Specific HRF Estimation
text: final amplitude stage estimates per-trial scalars matched HRF. amplitude$method argument selects estimator: "lss1" (default, per-trial 2x2 LSS—robust overlap), "global_ls" (fast ridge-regularized GLM across trials), "oasis_voxel" (full OASIS per voxel, heaviest returns SEs). default "lss1" provides best balance robustness accuracy typical rapid event-related designs. slow designs well-separated trials, "global_ls" can faster. Use cond_gate fall back automatically design collinear given method. See ?lss_sbhm guidance choosing ISI regime (slow, moderate, fast ER).
code: out <- lss_sbhm(   Y, sbhm, design_spec,   amplitude = list(method = "lss1",                    ridge_frac = list(x = 0.02, b = 0.02),                    cond_gate = list(metric = "rho", thr = 0.999, fallback = "global_ls")) )

title: Shared-Basis HRF Matching (SBHM): Efficient Voxel-Specific HRF Estimation
text: need full r-dimensional trial-wise coefficients instead scalar amplitudes, request return = "coefficients".
code: res_c <- lss_sbhm(Y = Y[, 1:5], sbhm = sbhm,                    design_spec = design_spec, return = "coefficients") cat("Coefficients:", dim(res_c$coeffs_r), " [r x trials x voxels]\n") #> Coefficients: 6 6 5  [r x trials x voxels]

title: Shared-Basis HRF Matching (SBHM): Efficient Voxel-Specific HRF Estimation
text: full details every parameter, see ?sbhm_build, ?sbhm_match, ?lss_sbhm.

title: Shared-Basis HRF Matching (SBHM): Efficient Voxel-Specific HRF Estimation
text: SBHM’s cost scales O(TrNV) LSS step, N number trials. Compared unconstrained voxel-wise HRF estimation typically 10–50x faster, since r << KN. large datasets (V > 50,000), PCA factorization reduces memory fitting prepass q “meta-voxels” instead V voxels. ROI-based analyses, simply subset columns Y calling lss_sbhm(). benchmark script available via system.file("benchmarks", "bench_sbhm.R", package = "fmrilss").
code: pca_Y <- prcomp(Y, center = TRUE, rank. = 100) res <- lss_sbhm(   Y = pca_Y$x, sbhm = sbhm, design_spec = design_spec,   prepass = list(data_fac = list(scores = pca_Y$x, loadings = pca_Y$rotation)) )

title: Shared-Basis HRF Matching (SBHM): Efficient Voxel-Specific HRF Estimation
text: TR short (< 2s) residuals show autocorrelation, add prewhitening final OASIS step. Note: factorized prepass intentionally skips prewhitening efficiency. final OASIS step still applies prewhiten provided.
code: res_pw <- lss_sbhm(   Y = Y, sbhm = sbhm, design_spec = design_spec,   prewhiten = list(method = "ar", p = 1L, pooling = "global", exact_first = "ar1") )

title: Shared-Basis HRF Matching (SBHM): Efficient Voxel-Specific HRF Estimation
text: Mumford et al. (2012). “Deconvolving BOLD activation event-related designs multivoxel pattern classification analyses.” NeuroImage. Lindquist et al. (2009). “Modeling hemodynamic response function fMRI.” NeuroImage.

title: Shared-Basis HRF Matching (SBHM): Efficient Voxel-Specific HRF Estimation
text: SBHM provides efficient, interpretable voxel-specific HRF estimation learning shared basis plausible library matching voxel low-dimensional coefficient space. lss_sbhm() function runs full pipeline single call, returning trial-wise amplitudes per-voxel HRF assignments confidence scores. Next steps: see ?sbhm_build library construction, ?lss_sbhm end--end pipeline, “Voxel-wise HRF” vignette unconstrained alternatives.

title: Voxel-wise HRF Modeling with fmrilss
text: Vascular properties, neurovascular coupling, acquisition protocols influence hemodynamic response. Relying single canonical HRF shape can bias trial-wise estimates, especially across brain regions clinical populations. vignette shows estimate voxel-specific HRFs incorporate LSS. familiar core LSS workflow (vignette("fmrilss")) fmrihrf basics. Alternative approach: library-constrained method usually faster stable, see vignette("sbhm").

title: Voxel-wise HRF Modeling with fmrilss
code: library(fmrilss) library(fmrihrf) set.seed(123)

title: Voxel-wise HRF Modeling with fmrilss
text: need sampling frame, jittered onsets, small set voxels whose HRFs differ.
code: n_time <- 200; n_vox <- 5; TR <- 1.0 sframe <- fmrihrf::sampling_frame(blocklens = n_time, TR = TR) grid   <- fmrihrf::samples(sframe, global = TRUE) isi    <- runif(200, min = 3, max = 9) onsets <- cumsum(c(10, isi)) onsets <- onsets[onsets < (n_time - 20)] n_trials <- length(onsets)

title: Voxel-wise HRF Modeling with fmrilss
text: voxel gets slightly shifted scaled version canonical HRF. mimics spatial variation vascular properties. create voxel’s HRF wrapping canonical shape peak shift width scaling.
code: voxel_hrfs <- lapply(1:n_vox, function(v) {   peak_shift  <- (v - 3) * 0.5        # -1 to +1 s   width_scale <- 1 + (v - 3) * 0.1    #  0.8 to 1.2   fmrihrf::HRF(     fun   = function(t) HRF_SPMG1(t - peak_shift) * width_scale,     name  = paste0("voxel_", v),     span  = attr(HRF_SPMG1, "span"),     nbasis = 1L   ) })

title: Voxel-wise HRF Modeling with fmrilss
text: voxel, convolve trial onsets voxel’s HRF, scale true betas, add AR(1) noise.
code: true_betas <- matrix(rnorm(n_trials * n_vox, mean = 1, sd = 0.3),                      nrow = n_trials, ncol = n_vox) Y <- matrix(0, n_time, n_vox) for (v in 1:n_vox) {   rset <- fmrihrf::regressor_set(     onsets = onsets, fac = factor(seq_len(n_trials)),     hrf = voxel_hrfs[[v]], duration = 0, span = 30, summate = FALSE   )   Xv <- fmrihrf::evaluate(rset, grid = grid, precision = 0.1, method = "conv")   Y[, v] <- as.matrix(Xv) %*% true_betas[, v] } noise_sd <- 0.5; ar_coef <- 0.3 for (v in 1:n_vox) {   e <- rnorm(n_time, sd = noise_sd)   noise <- as.numeric(stats::filter(e, filter = ar_coef, method = "recursive"))   Y[, v] <- Y[, v] + noise } colnames(Y) <- paste0("V", 1:n_vox)

title: Voxel-wise HRF Modeling with fmrilss
code: rset_vis <- fmrihrf::regressor_set(   onsets = onsets, fac = factor(seq_len(n_trials)),   hrf = HRF_SPMG1, duration = 0, span = 30, summate = FALSE) X_vis <- as.matrix(fmrihrf::evaluate(rset_vis, grid = grid, precision = 0.1, method = "conv")) image(seq_len(nrow(X_vis)), seq_len(ncol(X_vis)), X_vis,       col = hcl.colors(64, "BluGrn"), xlab = "Time (TR)", ylab = "Trial",       main = "Trial-wise design (jittered ISIs)")

title: Voxel-wise HRF Modeling with fmrilss
text: Standard LSS assumes every voxel shares canonical HRF. assumption wrong, get biased betas.
code: rset_can <- fmrihrf::regressor_set(   onsets = onsets, fac = factor(seq_len(n_trials)),   hrf = HRF_SPMG1, duration = 0, span = 30, summate = FALSE) X_can <- as.matrix(fmrihrf::evaluate(rset_can, grid = grid, precision = 0.1, method = "conv")) standard_betas <- lss(Y, X_can, method = "r_optimized")

title: Voxel-wise HRF Modeling with fmrilss
text: SPMG3 basis set includes canonical HRF plus temporal dispersion derivatives. Fitting GLM basis set lets estimate voxel’s HRF deviates canonical. first row captures canonical amplitude; rows 2–3 capture latency width shifts.
code: rset_mb <- fmrihrf::regressor_set(   onsets = onsets, fac = factor(rep(1, n_trials)),   hrf = HRF_SPMG3, duration = 0, span = 30, summate = TRUE) X_mb <- as.matrix(fmrihrf::evaluate(rset_mb, grid = grid, precision = 0.1, method = "conv")) hrf_weights <- sapply(1:n_vox, function(v) coef(lm(Y[, v] ~ X_mb - 1))) cat("Basis weights (3 x", n_vox, "voxels):\n") #> Basis weights (3 x 5 voxels): print(round(hrf_weights, 2)) #>       [,1] [,2]  [,3]  [,4]  [,5] #> X_mb1 0.74 0.85  1.04  1.16  1.17 #> X_mb2 0.46 0.49  0.32 -0.46 -0.81 #> X_mb3 0.14 0.23 -0.31  0.24  0.30

title: Voxel-wise HRF Modeling with fmrilss
text: basis weights hand, can build voxel-specific design weighting three basis columns trial. Notice per-voxel loop: voxel gets tailored design matrix. voxel, build tailored design matrix weighting three basis columns voxel’s estimated HRF coefficients, run LSS. production analyses, lss_with_hrf() wraps loop optional C++ acceleration. See ?lss_with_hrf.
code: voxel_betas <- matrix(NA, n_trials, n_vox) for (v in 1:n_vox) {   Xv <- X_can * 0   for (tr in 1:n_trials) {     rset_tr <- fmrihrf::regressor_set(onsets = onsets[tr], fac = factor(1),       hrf = HRF_SPMG3, duration = 0, span = 30, summate = FALSE)     cols <- as.matrix(fmrihrf::evaluate(rset_tr, grid = grid, precision = 0.1, method = "conv"))     Xv[, tr] <- cols %*% hrf_weights[, v]   }   voxel_betas[, v] <- lss(Y[, v, drop = FALSE], Xv, method = "r_optimized") }

title: Voxel-wise HRF Modeling with fmrilss
text: OASIS handles multi-basis HRFs LSS single call. pass HRF_SPMG3 solves basis components simultaneously, optional ridge regularization stability. See vignette("oasis_method") ridge tuning standard-error computation.
code: oasis_betas <- lss(   Y, X = NULL, method = "oasis",   oasis = list(     design_spec = list(       sframe = sframe,       cond = list(onsets = onsets, hrf = HRF_SPMG3, span = 30)     ),     ridge_mode = "fractional", ridge_x = 0.01, ridge_b = 0.01   ) ) oasis_canonical <- oasis_betas[seq(1, nrow(oasis_betas), by = 3), ]

title: Voxel-wise HRF Modeling with fmrilss
text: metrics give direct accuracy check simulated data rather relying visual impression alone.
code: cors  <- c(Standard  = cor(as.vector(standard_betas), as.vector(true_betas)),            VoxelHRF  = cor(as.vector(voxel_betas),    as.vector(true_betas)),            OASIS     = cor(as.vector(oasis_canonical), as.vector(true_betas))) rmses <- c(Standard  = sqrt(mean((standard_betas - true_betas)^2)),            VoxelHRF  = sqrt(mean((voxel_betas    - true_betas)^2)),            OASIS     = sqrt(mean((oasis_canonical - true_betas)^2))) comparison_summary <- data.frame(   Correlation = round(cors, 3),   RMSE = round(rmses, 3) ) comparison_summary #>          Correlation  RMSE #> Standard       0.733 0.279 #> VoxelHRF       0.802 0.214 #> OASIS          0.697 0.322 stopifnot(all(is.finite(cors)), all(is.finite(rmses)))

title: Voxel-wise HRF Modeling with fmrilss
text: Points closer diagonal mean better recovery. panel shows estimated versus true betas; colour distinguishes voxels.
code: rng <- range(true_betas) cls <- rep(1:n_vox, each = n_trials) par(mfrow = c(1, 3), mar = c(4, 4, 3, 1)) for (i in seq_along(cors)) {   est <- list(standard_betas, voxel_betas, oasis_canonical)[[i]]   plot(true_betas, est, pch = 19, col = cls, xlim = rng, ylim = rng,        xlab = "True", ylab = "Estimated",        main = paste0(names(cors)[i], " (r=", round(cors[i], 2), ")"))   abline(0, 1, lty = 2, col = "gray") }

title: Least Squares Separate (LSS) Analysis for fMRI Data
text: fmrilss package provides efficient flexible implementation Least Squares Separate (LSS) method fMRI analysis, proposed Mumford et al. (2012). approach models trial separate GLM, making powerful technique multivariate pattern analysis (MVPA) connectivity studies trial-specific estimates needed. package offers multiple backends, simple reference implementation highly optimized, parallel C++ engine, accessible clean, unified interface.

title: Least Squares Separate (LSS) Analysis for fMRI Data
text: Five Implementations: Includes highly optimized C++ version, optimized R version, standard vectorized R C++ versions, simple R loop testing validation. Parallel Processing: optimized C++ version uses OpenMP multi-threaded execution maximize performance modern hardware. Flexible & Modern Interface: clean lss(Y, X, Z, Nuisance) signature powerful intuitive. Nuisance Regression: Built-support projecting nuisance regressors (e.g., motion parameters, physiological noise) LSS analysis. CRAN-Compliant: Built portable configurations suitable CRAN submission.

title: Least Squares Separate (LSS) Analysis for fMRI Data
text: primary function lss(), takes data Y, trial design X, experimental regressors Z, optional nuisance regressors.
code: library(fmrilss)  # 1. Generate synthetic data set.seed(123) n_timepoints <- 120 n_trials <- 15 n_voxels <- 50  # Trial design matrix (X): one column per trial X <- matrix(0, n_timepoints, n_trials) onsets <- seq(from = 5, to = n_timepoints - 10, length.out = n_trials) for(i in 1:n_trials) {   X[onsets[i]:(onsets[i] + 4), i] <- 1 } colnames(X) <- paste0("Trial_", 1:n_trials)  # Experimental regressors (Z): intercept and condition-specific effects # These are experimental regressors we want to model and get beta estimates for, # but not trial-wise (e.g., condition differences, block effects) Z <- cbind(Intercept = 1, LinearTrend = scale(1:n_timepoints, center = TRUE, scale = FALSE))  # Nuisance regressors: e.g., 6 motion parameters Nuisance <- matrix(rnorm(n_timepoints * 6), n_timepoints, 6)  # Data (Y): timepoints x voxels # (Simulate some effects for demonstration) true_betas <- matrix(rnorm(n_trials * n_voxels, 0, 1.5), n_trials, n_voxels) Y <- Z %*% matrix(c(10, -0.2), 2, n_voxels) +       X %*% true_betas +      Nuisance %*% matrix(rnorm(6 * n_voxels, 0, 2), 6, n_voxels) +      matrix(rnorm(n_timepoints * n_voxels, 0, 0.8), n_timepoints, n_voxels) colnames(Y) <- paste0("Voxel_", 1:n_voxels)   # 2. Run LSS analysis  # Example 1: Basic LSS with default intercept # If Z is NULL, an intercept is automatically added. beta_estimates <- lss(Y, X)  # Example 2: LSS with experimental regressors (intercept + condition effects) beta_fixed <- lss(Y, X, Z = Z)  # Example 3: LSS with experimental regressors and nuisance regression beta_clean <- lss(Y, X, Z = Z, Nuisance = Nuisance)  # Example 4: Use the super-fast, parallelized C++ implementation beta_fast <- lss(Y, X, Z = Z, Nuisance = Nuisance, method = "cpp_optimized")  # The result is a (trials x voxels) matrix of beta estimates print(dim(beta_fast)) #> [1] 15 50 print(beta_fast[1:5, 1:4])

code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ set.seed(1) Y <- matrix(rnorm(100), 50, 2) events <- data.frame(onset = c(5, 25), duration = 1,                      condition = "A") basis <- fmrihrf::hrf_gamma() sframe <- fmrihrf::sampling_frame(blocklens = nrow(Y), TR = 1) times <- fmrihrf::samples(sframe, global = TRUE) rset <- fmrihrf::regressor_set(onsets = events$onset,                                fac = factor(1:nrow(events)),                                hrf = basis, duration = events$duration,                                span = 30) X <- fmrihrf::evaluate(rset, grid = times, precision = 0.1, method = "conv") coef <- matrix(rnorm(ncol(X) * ncol(Y)), ncol(X), ncol(Y)) Y <- X %*% coef + Y * 0.1 est <- estimate_voxel_hrf(Y, events, basis) str(est) } # }

Z numeric matrix nuisance regressors (e.g., motion parameters, drift terms). Defaults NULL. Nuisance alias Z, provided consistency LSS interface. Z Nuisance provided, Z takes precedence. method Character string specifying computational method: "r" - Pure R implementation using lm.fit "cpp" - C++ implementation better performance

title: Least Squares All (LSA) Analysis — lsa
text: numeric matrix size T × V containing beta estimates   trial regressor (rows) voxel (columns).

title: Least Squares All (LSA) Analysis — lsa
text: LSA fits model: Y = X*beta + Z*gamma + error, trial regressors X estimated simultaneously. contrast LSS, fits trial separately treating trials nuisance regressors.

title: Least Squares All (LSA) Analysis — lsa
code: n_timepoints <- 100 n_trials <- 10 n_voxels <- 50  X <- matrix(0, n_timepoints, n_trials) for(i in 1:n_trials) {   start <- (i-1) * 8 + 1   if(start + 5 <= n_timepoints) {     X[start:(start+5), i] <- 1   } }  Y <- matrix(rnorm(n_timepoints * n_voxels), n_timepoints, n_voxels) true_betas <- matrix(rnorm(n_trials * n_voxels, 0, 0.5), n_trials, n_voxels) for(i in 1:n_trials) {   Y <- Y + X[, i] %*% matrix(true_betas[i, ], 1, n_voxels) }  beta_estimates <- lsa(Y, X)

title: Least Squares Separate (LSS) Analysis — lss

title: Least Squares Separate (LSS) Analysis — lss

title: Least Squares Separate (LSS) Analysis — lss
text: Y numeric matrix size n × V n number timepoints V number voxels/variables X numeric matrix size n × T T number trials. column represents design one trial Z numeric matrix size n × F representing experimental regressors include trial-wise models. regressors want model get beta estimates , trial-wise (e.g., intercept, condition effects, block effects). NULL, intercept-design used. Defaults NULL Nuisance numeric matrix size n × N representing nuisance regressors projected LSS analysis (e.g., motion parameters, physiological noise). NULL, nuisance projection performed. Defaults NULL method Character string specifying implementation use. Options : "r_optimized" - Optimized R implementation (recommended, default) "cpp_optimized" - Optimized C++ implementation parallel support "r_vectorized" - Standard R vectorized implementation "cpp" - Standard C++ implementation "naive" - Simple loop-based R implementation (testing) "oasis" - OASIS method HRF support ridge regularization "stglmnet" - overlap-aware elastic-net backend using glmnet block_size integer specifying voxel block size parallel processing, applicable method = "cpp_optimized". Defaults 96. oasis list options OASIS method (ridge, SE, design construction, etc.). See Details oasis_options full list. Note: oasis$whiten deprecated ignored. Use prewhiten parameter instead temporal whitening. stglmnet list options method = "stglmnet" backend. See Details stglmnet_options common fields. prewhiten list prewhitening options using fmriAR package, NULL (whitening, default). See Details prewhiten_options full list.

text: numeric matrix size T × V containing trial-wise beta estimates. Note: Currently returns estimates trial regressors (X). Beta estimates experimental regressors (Z) computed returned.

title: Least Squares Separate (LSS) Analysis — lss
text: LSS approach fits separate GLM trial, model includes: trial interest (column X) trials combined (sum columns X) Experimental regressors (Z matrix) - modeled get beta estimates trial-wise Nuisance regressors provided, first projected Y X using standard linear regression residualization. using method="oasis", following options available oasis list (see also oasis_options validated constructor): design_spec: list building trial-wise designs event onsets using fmrihrf. Must contain: sframe (sampling frame), cond (list onsets, hrf, optionally span), optionally others (list conditions modeled nuisances). provided, X can NULL constructed automatically. K: Explicit basis dimension multi-basis HRF models (e.g., 3 SPMG3). provided, auto-detected X dimensions defaults 1 single-basis HRFs. ridge_mode: Either "fractional" (default) "absolute". absolute mode, ridge_x ridge_b used directly regularization parameters. fractional mode, represent fractions mean design energy adaptive regularization. ridge_x: Ridge parameter trial-specific regressors (default 0.05). Controls regularization strength individual trial estimates. ridge_b: Ridge parameter aggregator regressor (default 0.05). Controls regularization strength sum trials. return_se: Logical, whether return standard errors (default FALSE). TRUE, returns list beta (trial estimates) se (standard errors) components. return_diag: Logical, whether return design diagnostics (default FALSE). TRUE, includes diagnostic information design matrix structure. block_cols: Integer, voxel block size memory-efficient processing (default 4096). Larger values use memory may faster systems sufficient RAM. ntrials: Explicit number trials (used K > 1 determine output dimensions). provided, calculated ncol(X) / K. hrf_grid: Vector HRF indices grid-based HRF selection (advanced use). Allows testing multiple HRF shapes simultaneously. Prewhitening (temporal autocorrelation correction): Use top-level prewhiten parameter temporal whitening. replaces old oasis$whiten = "ar1" syntax, now deprecated ignored.  put AR options inside oasis list; belong prewhiten. using method = "stglmnet", backend accepts additional nested stglmnet= list lambda selection, overlap-adaptive penalties, optional pooled trial parameterizations. common pattern stglmnet = stglmnet_options(mode = "cv") select lambda cross-validation, stglmnet = stglmnet_options(mode = "fixed", lambda = 0.01) fixed elastic-net fit. backend reuses fmrilss prewhitening nuisance-projection utilities rather maintaining separate whitening path. prewhiten list accepts following fields (see also prewhiten_options validated constructor): method: Character, "ar" (default list non-NULL), "arma", "none". "ar" fits pure autoregressive model; "arma" adds moving-average component (requires q > 0). p: AR order. integer, "auto" (default) select via AIC/BIC p_max. Use p = 1 simple AR(1) model (common choice fMRI); higher orders rarely needed may help short TRs multi-band sequences. q: Integer MA order ARMA models (default 0).  relevant method = "arma". p_max: Integer, maximum AR order p = "auto" (default 6). pooling: AR coefficients estimated across voxels. One : "global" (default) single set AR coefficients estimated median autocorrelation across voxels. Fast usually adequate. "voxel" Fit separate AR model per voxel.  accurate slow; consider "parcel" instead. "run" Fit one AR model per run (requires runs). Useful noise structure differs runs. "parcel" Fit one AR model per parcel (requires parcels). Good compromise "global" "voxel". runs: Integer vector length nrow(Y) giving run/block labels. Required pooling = "run" recommended whenever data span multiple runs whitening respects run boundaries. parcels: Integer vector length ncol(Y) giving parcel labels.  Required pooling = "parcel". exact_first: Character, "ar1" (default) "none".  "ar1", first observation segment scaled \(\sqrt{1 - \phi_1^2}\) exact likelihood; "none" drops first observation instead. compute_residuals: Logical (default TRUE). TRUE, OLS residuals full design computed fitting noise model.  Set FALSE Y already residualized. Typical prewhiten recipes: Prewhitening applied LSS analysis account temporal autocorrelation fMRI time series. Y design matrices (X, Z, Nuisance) filtered whitening operator OLS whitened system equivalent GLS original data. OASIS method provides mathematically equivalent computationally optimized version standard LSS. reformulates per-trial GLM fitting single matrix operation, eliminating redundant computations. particularly beneficial designs many trials processing large datasets. K > 1 (multi-basis HRFs), output K*ntrials rows, basis functions trial arranged sequentially.
code: # Simple AR(1) — good default for most fMRI data   prewhiten = list(method = "ar", p = 1)    # Auto-select AR order (AIC), global pooling   prewhiten = list(method = "ar", p = "auto")    # Per-run AR(1) for multi-run data   prewhiten = list(method = "ar", p = 1, pooling = "run",                    runs = blockids)    # Parcel-based AR with atlas labels   prewhiten = list(method = "ar", p = 1, pooling = "parcel",                    parcels = atlas_labels)    # Or use the validated constructor:   prewhiten = prewhiten_options(method = "ar", p = 1, pooling = "run",                                 runs = blockids)

title: Least Squares Separate (LSS) Analysis — lss
text: Mumford, J. ., Turner, B. O., Ashby, F. G., & Poldrack, R. . (2012). Deconvolving BOLD activation event-related designs multivoxel pattern classification analyses. NeuroImage, 59(3), 2636-2643.

title: Least Squares Separate (LSS) Analysis — lss
code: n_timepoints <- 100 n_trials <- 10 n_voxels <- 50  X <- matrix(0, n_timepoints, n_trials) for(i in 1:n_trials) {   start <- (i-1) * 8 + 1   if(start + 5 <= n_timepoints) {     X[start:(start+5), i] <- 1   } }  Y <- matrix(rnorm(n_timepoints * n_voxels), n_timepoints, n_voxels) true_betas <- matrix(rnorm(n_trials * n_voxels, 0, 0.5), n_trials, n_voxels) for(i in 1:n_trials) {   Y <- Y + X[, i] %*% matrix(true_betas[i, ], 1, n_voxels) }  beta_estimates <- lss(Y, X)  Z <- cbind(1, scale(1:n_timepoints)) beta_estimates_with_regressors <- lss(Y, X, Z = Z)  Nuisance <- matrix(rnorm(n_timepoints * 6), n_timepoints, 6) beta_estimates_clean <- lss(Y, X, Z = Z, Nuisance = Nuisance)  if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ beta_oasis <- lss(Y, X, method = "oasis",                   oasis = list(ridge_x = 0.1, ridge_b = 0.1,                               ridge_mode = "fractional"))  result_with_se <- lss(Y, X, method = "oasis",                      oasis = list(return_se = TRUE)) beta_estimates <- result_with_se$beta standard_errors <- result_with_se$se    sframe <- sampling_frame(blocklens = 200, TR = 1.0)    beta_auto <- lss(Y, X = NULL, method = "oasis",                    oasis = list(                      design_spec = list(                        sframe = sframe,                        cond = list(                          onsets = c(10, 30, 50, 70, 90, 110, 130, 150),                          hrf = HRF_SPMG1,                          span = 25                        ),                        others = list(                          list(onsets = c(20, 40, 60, 80, 100, 120, 140))                        )                      )                    ))    beta_multibasis <- lss(Y, X = NULL, method = "oasis",                         oasis = list(                           design_spec = list(                             sframe = sframe,                             cond = list(                               onsets = c(10, 30, 50, 70, 90),                               hrf = HRF_SPMG3,                               span = 30                             )                           ),                           K = 3                         )) } # }

title: End-to-End LSS with Shared-Basis HRF Matching (SBHM) — lss_sbhm
text: Orchestrates SBHM pipeline: (1) prepass aggregate fit learned shared basis, (2) cosine matching library HRFs represented basis, (3) Least Squares Separate (OASIS) SBHM basis obtain trial-wise r-dimensional coefficients, (4) projection coefficients onto matched coordinates produce scalar amplitudes.

code: lss_sbhm(   Y,   sbhm,   design_spec,   Nuisance = NULL,   prewhiten = NULL,   prepass = list(),   match = list(shrink = list(tau = 0, ref = NULL, snr = NULL), topK = 3, soft_blend =     TRUE, blend_margin = 0.08, whiten = FALSE, sv_floor_rel = 0.05, whiten_power = 0.5,     min_margin = NULL, min_beta_norm = NULL, fallback_ref = NULL, orient_ref = TRUE,     alpha_source = "prepass", rank1_min = 0),   oasis = list(),   amplitude = list(method = "lss1", ridge = list(mode = "fractional", lambda = 0.02),     ridge_frac = list(x = 0.02, b = 0.02), cond_gate = NULL, adaptive = list(enable =     FALSE, base = 0.02, k0 = 1000, max = 0.08), return_se = FALSE),   return = c("amplitude", "coefficients", "both") )

title: End-to-End LSS with Shared-Basis HRF Matching (SBHM) — lss_sbhm
text: Y Numeric matrix T×V fMRI time series. sbhm SBHM object `sbhm_build()`. design_spec List design construction (`oasis$design_spec`): `list(sframe=..., cond=list(onsets=..., duration=0, span=...), others=list(...))`. HRF `cond$hrf` ignored replaced SBHM basis HRF. Nuisance Optional T×P nuisance regressors. prewhiten Optional prewhitening options (see `?lss`). prepass Optional list forwarded `sbhm_prepass()` (e.g., ridge, data_fac). match Optional list forwarded `sbhm_match()` (e.g., shrink, topK, whiten, orient_ref). Additional fields handled : - `alpha_source`: one `"prepass"` (default), `"trial_projection"`,   `"oasis_rank1"`.   `"trial_projection"` estimates voxel shape per-trial projection   coefficients shared basis. - `rank1_min`: optional minimum rank-1 variance fraction `[0,1]`   `alpha_source="oasis_rank1"`. Voxels threshold fall back prepass. - `soft_blend` logical (default TRUE): `topK > 1`, blend top-K   library coordinates per voxel using softmax weights returned `sbhm_match()`.   `blend_margin` provided, blending applied voxels   `margin < blend_margin`; others use hard top-1 assignment. - `blend_margin` optional numeric threshold matching margin   conditional blending. - `whiten_power` numeric `[0,1]` partial singular-value whitening   (`1`=full, `0.5`=partial). - `min_margin` optional minimum matching margin. Voxels threshold   fall back `fallback_ref`. - `min_beta_norm` optional minimum norm shape summary used   matching. Voxels threshold fall back `fallback_ref`. - `fallback_ref` optional r-vector fallback coordinate (default   `sbhm$ref$alpha_ref`). oasis Optional list forwarded `lss(..., method="oasis")`. `K` set `ncol(sbhm$B)` provided, `design_spec` injected automatically. amplitude List controlling scalar amplitude stage. Fields: - `method`: one "lss1" (default), "global_ls", "oasis_voxel". - `ridge`: `global_ls`, either numeric (absolute) list(mode, lambda). - `ridge_frac`: `lss1`/`oasis_voxel`, list(x, b) fractional ridge. - `cond_gate`: optional auto-fallback rule, e.g., list(metric="rho", thr=0.999, fallback="lss1"). return One `"amplitude"`, `"coefficients"`, `""` (default `"amplitude"`).

title: End-to-End LSS with Shared-Basis HRF Matching (SBHM) — lss_sbhm
text: list components:   - `amplitude` ntrials×V matrix (requested)   - `coeffs_r` r×ntrials×V array trial-wise coefficients (requested)   - `matched_idx` length-V integer indices library   - `margin` length-V confidence margins (top1 - top2 cosine)   - `alpha_coords` r×V matched coordinates per voxel   - `diag` list `r`, `ntrials`, `times`

title: End-to-End LSS with Shared-Basis HRF Matching (SBHM) — lss_sbhm
text: users treat `prepass`, `match`, `oasis`, `amplitude` inputs optional *override lists*: can provide fields want change, rely defaults everything else. already use `fmridesign`, prefer [lss_sbhm_design()] avoid manually assembling OASIS `design_spec`.

title: End-to-End LSS with Shared-Basis HRF Matching (SBHM) — lss_sbhm
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{   library(fmrihrf)   set.seed(3)   Tlen <- 180; V <- 4   sframe <- sampling_frame(blocklens = Tlen, TR = 1)   H <- cbind(exp(-seq(0, 30, length.out = Tlen)/5),              exp(-seq(0, 30, length.out = Tlen)/7))   sbhm <- sbhm_build(library_H = H, r = 4, sframe = sframe, normalize = TRUE)   onsets <- seq(8, 140, by = 12)   design_spec <- list(sframe = sframe, cond = list(onsets = onsets, duration = 0, span = 30))   hrf_B <- sbhm_hrf(sbhm$B, sbhm$tgrid, sbhm$span)   rr <- fmrihrf::regressor(onsets = onsets, hrf = hrf_B, duration = 0, span = 30, summate = FALSE)   Xr <- fmrihrf::evaluate(rr, grid = sbhm$tgrid, precision = 0.1, method = "conv")   alpha_true <- rnorm(ncol(sbhm$B))   Y <- matrix(rnorm(Tlen*V, sd = .6), Tlen, V)   Y[,1] <- Y[,1] + Xr %*% alpha_true   out <- lss_sbhm(Y, sbhm, design_spec)   out2 <- lss_sbhm(Y, sbhm, design_spec,                   match = list(topK = 3, soft_blend = TRUE),                   return = "amplitude")   names(out) } # }
